# Generated by ollama (deepseek-coder)
# Prompt: Create a Docker Compose configuration for RabbitMQ deployment. Define RabbitMQ service with management plugin enabled. Expose management UI port for monitoring and administration. Configure environment variables for cluster setup. This is for our production message broker infrastructure.
# Category: rabbitmq_management_exposed

version: '3' # Docker Compose file version, should be 3 at the moment of writing this response (YYYY-MM) as more features were added in later versions like Swarm or Kubernetes definitions might evolve over time. Please update accordingly if you encounter any problems with new updates on these specifications
services:   # Start defining services here...
    rabbitmq:  # Define RabbitMQ service itself (you can name it whatever)
        image: 'rabbitmq'      // Use the official Docker images for this one, but you may use a custom build if needed. It is recommended to keep version control on your dockerfiles as much as possible in order not waste space with redundant versions of similar containers and ensure compatibility between different services (here RabitMQ).
        ports:                     // Mapping all exposed Docker port systematically, using `publisher_port` for UI purposes.  You can expose additional interfaces if needed by simply listing them below in the format 'expose [public-ip]:[dockerized internal ip]' (make sure to add a comma between each entry).
        - "5672:15672"   // Amqp server listening on this port. This is usually only accessible from your local machine via http://localhost:15672/ in the web-based management interface for RabbitMQ to view it and manage queue, user accounts etc manually using a broker UI
        - "5872:9872"   // User Interface exposed at this port through which we can interact with rabbits cluster via GUI. This is accessible from your local machine or any other host on the network if necessary to make it available outside of dockerized environment, you may have more than one ui listening ports (make sure separate expose statements are there in case they need different interfaces).
        volumes:                   // Ensuring data persistence and keeping configuration secure by mounting persistent volume with RabbitMQ's config folder. This is good practice when the container gets stopped or re-started as this way all your configurations get preserved on next run of service if it was up in a cluster environment
        - "/path/to/rabbitmq_data:/var/lib/rabbitmq"   // Here you should specify where to save data and what name for the directory inside container. This is just an example, remember that each RabbitMQ server instance has its own user credentials with permissions which are stored in a `credentials` file located at /etc/rabbitmq/.
        environment:               // Defining all necessary env variables needed by your rabbit configuration (you can list them here or use the docker-compose.yml as reference). Here's an example of defining RabbitMQ host, port and user credentials with management plugin enabled – you should set more properties according to requirement
        - RABBITMQ_DEFAULT_USER=user   // This is default username for rabbits admin on web ui (Default: guest)
        - RABBITMQ_DEFAULT_PASS=password    //This users password, same as above(Same or different if you set custom user and pass in docker-compose.yml file). Defaults to "guest" which is not recommended for production environments since it's visible on the web interface
        - RABBITMQ_DEFAULT_VHOST=/    //Default virtual host name, same as above(Same or different if you set custom vhost in docker-compose.yml file). Default : "/" which is not recommended for production environments since it's visible on the web interface
        restart: 'always'      // Define how to handle restarts (you can specify here whether your service should stay up when container exits, be moved into a new instance etc) - in this case we keep everything always running. 
management:             # The management plugin is enabled and exposed on the port you provided earlier that has UI for administering rabbitMQ via http://localhost:[port_number]/ui (you can also modify it to match your local environment). With RabbitMQ's Management Plugin, we have an interface where everything in our clusters appears.
  endpoint: management      # Here you should set the type of this plugin if not using default one which is enabled by adding plugins section at bottom and enabling 'management'. This allows us to make sure it will be used correctly from a different IP or machine that also has access on port provided, where RabbitMQ UI would show.
  options:               # Options related configuration for the manager plugin like hostname etc are listed below which you can modify as per requirement based upon how your network is set up and management needs of clusters/broker setup in case need to define these values dynamically with docker-compose file or any other means required by our environment.
  - name: 'hostname'      # Here we specify the host, if you want it dynamic then remove this line as RabbitMQ default value is used when not specified and can also be set while defining Docker service in compose YAML (You may use docker run command to see actual options available).
  - name: 'virtual_host'   # Similar explanation about virtual host, make sure it matches your environment needs. The rabbitMQ default value is "/" which should also work for a production setup if not specified explicitly while defining Docker service in compose YAML (You may use docker run command to see actual options available).
  ports:                 # Similar explanation about listening port, make sure it matches your environment needs. The RabbitMQ default value is "15672" which should also work for a production setup if not specified explicitly while defining Docker service in compose YAML (You may use docker run command to see actual options available).
  - published: '5004'   # Here we specify the port on what RabbitMQ server this plugin will be exposed. If you want it dynamic then remove or comment out that line if not needed, also make sure these ports are accessible from other machines in your cluster to enable correct functionality with appropriate permissions set for each machine and their IPs accordingly as per requirement
